name: Stress Response Style
description: How do you handle pressure? Discover your natural stress response and what it reveals about you.

fighter.title: The Fighter
fighter.description: Under pressure, you activate. You push harder, take charge and confront challenges head-on. Stress makes you sharper, not slower. You are at your best when the stakes are high and action is required immediately.
fighter.strengths: When everyone else freezes, you move. Your ability to take decisive action under fire makes you invaluable in high-stakes situations.
fighter.challenges: Learn to recognise when aggression or over-control is making things worse. Not every problem responds to force — sometimes the best action is a pause.

planner.title: The Planner
planner.description: When stress hits, you reach for structure. Lists, plans, research and systems calm your nervous system. You need to understand a problem fully before you can feel okay about it. Preparation is your coping mechanism.
characteristics: Makes lists and breaks problems down
Needs time to think before responding
Can get stuck in analysis paralysis
planner.strengths: Your thorough approach means you rarely make panicked decisions you regret. Your plans work because you've genuinely thought them through.
planner.challenges: Sometimes a good-enough decision now beats a perfect decision too late. Practice tolerating uncertainty — not every problem can be fully solved in advance.

connector.title: The Connector
connector.description: Stress sends you toward people. Talking it through, being heard, getting perspective from others — connection is how you process and recover. You're at your best when you don't face hard things alone.
characteristics: Processes emotions by talking them out
Empathetic — also supports others when they're stressed
Strong social bonds are your resilience source
connector.strengths: Your ability to lean on and build strong support networks means you rarely carry burdens alone. You also show others they don't have to either.
connector.challenges: Develop some tools for when your support network is unavailable. Building inner resilience alongside your social resilience will make you more robust.

withdrawer.title: The Withdrawer
withdrawer.description: When the world gets too loud, you need to get quiet. Stress sends you inward — to solitude, reflection and recovery. You recharge by processing alone before you're ready to engage. You need space before you can act.
characteristics: Appears calm on the surface but feels deeply inside
Recharges through quiet and alone time
withdrawer.strengths: Your reflective nature means you rarely overreact or say things you regret. The clarity you find in solitude often produces the wisest responses.
withdrawer.challenges: Withdrawal can look like avoidance to others. Communicating that you need time — not that you're shutting people out — will protect your relationships during hard periods.

questions.question: Something unexpected goes wrong at work or home. Your first instinct is to:
options: Jump into action — start fixing it immediately
Sit down and make a plan before doing anything
Call or message someone you trust to talk it through
Step away and find somewhere quiet to gather your thoughts

questions.question: Under pressure with a deadline, you tend to:
options: Work faster and harder — pressure sharpens your focus
Break the task into steps and work through them systematically
Check in with teammates or colleagues more than usual
Work alone in a quiet space away from distractions

questions.question: After a very stressful day, what do you most need to recover?
options: Physical activity — a run, gym session or something to burn it off
Time to review what happened and plan how to handle it better
An evening with friends or family — talking and being around people
Complete alone time — silence, no obligations, nobody needing anything

questions.question: When you're overwhelmed, people around you would most likely notice:
options: You become more intense, direct or forceful than usual
You go very quiet and focused — head down, working through it
You reach out more — more messages, more check-ins, more conversations
You disappear — become harder to reach and need more space

questions.question: You get some unexpectedly bad news. Within the first hour, you:
options: Start taking action on whatever you can control immediately
Research and gather as much information as possible
Want to talk to someone about it as soon as possible
Need to sit with it privately before you can talk to anyone

questions.question: Your main coping mechanism when life gets hard is:
options: Channelling stress into productive work or physical energy
Organisation — sorting, cleaning, planning things you can control
Reaching out to your support network of trusted people
Creating space — cancelling non-essentials and protecting your energy

questions.question: Which statement best describes your stress pattern?
options: I feel better once I'm doing something — inaction is unbearable
I feel better once I understand the situation and have a plan
I feel better once I've talked it through with someone I trust
I feel better once I've had time completely alone to reset

questions.question: In a crisis involving a group of people, you naturally:
options: Step up, take charge and start directing action
Assess the situation carefully before suggesting a structured approach
Check on how everyone is feeling and make sure people are supported
Find a way to work independently on your part of the problem
